title: Atlas VPN Review 2026: Discontinued — Service Shut Down in April 2024
metaDescription: Atlas VPN shut down on April 24, 2024. All paid users were migrated to NordVPN. Read what happened, why it closed, and which VPN alternatives to use instead.
pros: Offered a genuinely usable free plan with 5 GB/month data and no ads
WireGuard support delivered fast speeds — averaging 317 Mbps across tested servers
Clean, simple apps that were easy to use for beginners
Affordable premium plans starting at $1.83/month on 3-year deals
Unlimited simultaneous device connections on premium plans
SafeSwap rotating IP technology for enhanced anonymity
Supported P2P torrenting even on the free tier
cons: Service permanently shut down on April 24, 2024 — no longer available
Critical Linux zero-day vulnerability in 2023 leaked users' real IP addresses
Small server network of roughly 1,000 servers in 49 locations
Free plan limited to only 4 server locations and 5 GB/month
Never published an independent no-logs audit
Short operational history — founded in 2019, shut down in 2024

testResults.noLogsClaim: Claimed but never independently audited; now irrelevant as service is shut down

speedLocal.note: Service discontinued April 24, 2024 — no current testing possible. Historical data from Cloudwards testing on 295 Mbps baseline.

speedLongDistance.download: 201 Mbps (historical US server test from EU)
speedLongDistance.note: Service discontinued April 24, 2024 — no current testing possible. Historical data from Cloudwards testing.

reviewContent:
## Atlas VPN Is Discontinued — Do Not Download It **Atlas VPN permanently shut down on April 24, 2024.** You cannot sign up, download the apps, or connect to any Atlas VPN servers. The service no longer exists in any form. If you landed on this page searching for an Atlas VPN review, here is what you need to know: Atlas VPN is dead. It was acquired by Nord Security in 2021, operated for roughly two and a half more years under that umbrella, and then got folded into NordVPN when the parent company decided three VPN brands was one too many. All paid subscribers were migrated to NordVPN. Free users were cut off entirely. This page covers the full history of Atlas VPN — what it was, what went wrong, how the shutdown played out, what happened to user data, and which VPN services you should consider instead. We keep this review live because people still search for Atlas VPN, and some third-party sites still rank outdated reviews that do not mention the shutdown. ## The Full Timeline: From Launch to Shutdown Atlas VPN had one of the shortest lifespans in the consumer VPN industry. Here is the complete chronology. **2019 — Launch.** Atlas VPN was founded by Peakstar Technologies Inc., a company registered in Wilmington, Delaware, USA. The initial pitch was straightforward: a freemium VPN with a legitimately usable free tier. Unlike most free VPNs that throttle bandwidth, inject ads, or harvest user data, Atlas VPN offered 5 GB of monthly data with no strings attached. The free plan included access to servers in three countries, WireGuard encryption, and even support for P2P torrenting. **2020-2021 — Rapid growth.** Atlas VPN gained traction through word of mouth and favorable coverage from VPN review sites. By mid-2021, the service had accumulated roughly 6 million users worldwide. The free tier was the primary growth engine — it was one of very few free VPNs that privacy-conscious reviewers could recommend without heavy caveats. **October 2021 — Acquired by Nord Security.** Nord Security, the parent company behind NordVPN and Surfshark, announced the acquisition of Atlas VPN. The stated goal was to expand Nord Security's reach into the freemium market. Atlas VPN was positioned as the entry-level brand: NordVPN for premium users, Surfshark for budget-conscious users, and Atlas VPN for people who wanted something free or very cheap. Nord Security promised that Atlas VPN would continue to operate independently. **July 2021 — SafeSwap introduced.** Before the acquisition closed, Atlas VPN launched SafeSwap, a feature that rotated users through multiple IP addresses on a single server connection. This was a genuine innovation at the time, designed to make traffic correlation attacks harder for surveillance actors. **2022-2023 — Under Nord Security.** Atlas VPN continued operating but received relatively few meaningful updates compared to NordVPN and Surfshark. The server network remained at roughly 1,000 servers in 49 locations — small by industry standards. Premium pricing dropped aggressively, with three-year plans hitting $1.83/month during promotional periods and even $1.64/month during the 2023 Black Friday sale. **September 5, 2023 — The Linux zero-day.** A security researcher discovered that Atlas VPN's Linux client (version 1.0.3) had a critical vulnerability. The Linux app ran an unauthenticated API endpoint on localhost port 8076. Any website a user visited could send a request to that endpoint, disconnect the VPN tunnel, and then determine the user's real IP address through a second request. The researcher initially tried to report the vulnerability to Atlas VPN through normal channels, received no response, and ultimately published the exploit code publicly on Reddit. Atlas VPN patched the issue in version 1.1, released on September 18, 2023 — nearly two weeks after public disclosure. No CVE was formally assigned. This incident was deeply damaging. A VPN's core job is to protect your IP address. An unauthenticated local API that lets any website defeat that protection is not a minor oversight — it suggests insufficient security review processes for a product whose entire value proposition is security. **March 25, 2024 — Shutdown announced.** Atlas VPN published an announcement titled "An important announcement from Atlas VPN" on its blog. The statement cited "insurmountable challenges" including rapidly advancing technological demands, a highly competitive market, and escalating costs of providing top-tier services. The effective shutdown date was set for April 24, 2024, giving users exactly 30 days. **April 24, 2024 — Service terminated.** Atlas VPN ceased all operations. Apps were removed from the Apple App Store and Google Play Store. The website stopped accepting new registrations. All servers were decommissioned. ## What Happened to Atlas VPN Users After the Shutdown The migration process varied depending on subscription type and platform. **Paid subscribers (direct billing):** All active paid accounts were automatically migrated to NordVPN. The only data transferred was the user's email address and remaining subscription length. No browsing history, connection logs, or other sensitive data was included — Atlas VPN did not collect such data (though this was never independently verified). The new NordVPN accounts had auto-renewal turned off by default, meaning users would not be charged by NordVPN unless they explicitly opted in after their Atlas VPN subscription period expired. **Apple App Store subscribers:** Users who subscribed through Apple's in-app purchase system were refunded for their remaining subscription time and had their accounts terminated on April 25, 2024. There was no automatic migration to NordVPN for these users — Apple's payment system made a seamless transfer impossible. **Google Play subscribers:** Google Play subscriptions were managed through the store to facilitate a smoother transition to NordVPN, though the exact mechanism differed from direct-billing migrations. **Free users:** Anyone on Atlas VPN's free tier simply lost access. There was no migration, no refund (obviously), and no special offer. Atlas VPN did advertise NordVPN discounts of up to 69% off for free users who wanted to switch, but this was standard NordVPN promotional pricing available to anyone. For paid users, the migration was objectively an upgrade. NordVPN offers over 7,000 servers in more than 118 countries, six independently audited no-logs reports (by PwC and Deloitte), post-quantum encryption, the proprietary NordLynx protocol built on WireGuard, and substantially better streaming unblocking. Going from Atlas VPN to NordVPN is like going from a Kia to a Mercedes at no extra cost. For free users, the shutdown was a genuine loss. Atlas VPN's free tier was one of the few honest free VPN options in the market, and its disappearance left a gap — though Proton VPN has since filled that gap convincingly. ## How Fast Was Atlas VPN? Historical Speed Test Data Atlas VPN can no longer be tested, but multiple independent reviewers published speed test data before the shutdown. Here is a compilation of the most reliable results. **Baseline connection:** 295 Mbps download / 94 Mbps upload | Server Location | Download (Mbps) | Upload (Mbps) | Ping (ms) | |----------------|----------------|---------------|----------| | UK | 279.4 | 90.2 | 60 | | Singapore | 255.5 | 5.2 | 196 | | South Africa | 257.9 | 2.7 | 273 | | Brazil | 235.8 | 1.3 | 246 | | Australia | 207.8 | 3.6 | 281 | | US | 201.4 | 7.7 | 130 | *Source: Cloudwards.net historical testing data* The global average across all tested locations was approximately 317 Mbps download according to Cybernews testing on a faster baseline, which placed Atlas VPN among the faster VPN services during its operational period. Speed retention on nearby servers reached 88-94%, dropping to around 63-68% on long-distance connections. All speed tests were conducted using the WireGuard protocol, which was Atlas VPN's default and recommended option. IKEv2/IPSec was available as an alternative but delivered noticeably slower results in every published benchmark. For context, these speeds were competitive with mid-tier VPNs but well below what NordVPN (900+ Mbps) and Surfshark (700+ Mbps) deliver in 2026 with their latest protocol optimizations.
📖 Keep Reading: Explore our Best VPNs of 2026 ranking or learn what a VPN is and why you need one.
## Security and Privacy: A Mixed Record Atlas VPN's security story is a tale of adequate basics undermined by a critical failure and missing verification. ### Encryption and Protocols Atlas VPN supported two VPN protocols: - **WireGuard** — The modern, lightweight protocol that delivered the best speeds. Atlas VPN used the standard WireGuard implementation with ChaCha20-Poly1305 encryption. - **IKEv2/IPSec** — The older, more established protocol used as a fallback. It used AES-256 encryption. Notably absent was OpenVPN, the industry workhorse that most VPN providers still support for compatibility with routers and legacy devices. Cloudwards listed OpenVPN in their review data, but most hands-on testers confirmed it was not available in the apps. ### Kill Switch and Leak Protection Atlas VPN included a network kill switch on Windows, macOS, iOS, and Android. DNS leak protection was enabled by default. In testing prior to the Linux zero-day incident, the kill switch generally functioned as expected on desktop and mobile platforms. ### The No-Logs Question Atlas VPN claimed a strict no-logs policy. The privacy policy stated that the service did not log browsing history, traffic destinations, DNS queries, or IP addresses. However — and this is the critical distinction — Atlas VPN never submitted to an independent security audit to verify these claims. Every major VPN competitor had been audited multiple times by the time Atlas VPN shut down: - **NordVPN**: 6 audits by PwC and Deloitte (2018-2025) - **Surfshark**: 2 audits by Deloitte (2023, 2024) - **ExpressVPN**: 3 audits by PwC and Cure53 - **Proton VPN**: 2 audits by Securitum - **Atlas VPN**: 0 audits For a VPN operated under US jurisdiction — a Five Eyes country where law enforcement can issue National Security Letters with gag orders — the absence of independent verification was a serious liability. ### The 2023 Linux Zero-Day in Detail The September 2023 vulnerability deserves specific attention because it illustrates a pattern of insufficient security practices. The Atlas VPN Linux client (v1.0.3) ran a local API server on port 8076 with no authentication whatsoever. This API had endpoints that could disconnect the VPN tunnel. Because the API listened on localhost and did not validate the origin of requests, any web page could fire off an AJAX request to `http://127.0.0.1:8076/connection/stop`, kill the VPN connection, and then run JavaScript to detect the user's real IP address through WebRTC or similar browser APIs. The attack required no special privileges, no malware installation, and no user interaction beyond visiting a malicious webpage. This is about as bad as a VPN vulnerability gets — a passive attack that defeats the VPN's entire purpose. The researcher who found the vulnerability attempted to report it to Atlas VPN through proper channels and received no response. After waiting, they published the full exploit on Reddit, which forced Atlas VPN to respond. A patch was released 13 days after public disclosure. In the security world, 13 days of public exposure for a VPN IP leak vulnerability is unacceptable. ## Streaming: Decent but Inconsistent Atlas VPN's streaming performance was a mixed bag that varied significantly by region and over time. **What worked (premium only):** - Netflix: Unblocked up to 11 regional libraries, including US, UK, Germany, France, Spain, Italy, and Canada - BBC iPlayer: Worked consistently through UK servers - Amazon Prime Video: Worked on US and UK servers - Hulu: Worked through US servers **What did not work:** - Disney+: Blocked on most servers - Netflix on free servers: Free tier could not unblock any streaming services - Results varied month to month as streaming platforms updated their VPN detection The free tier was essentially useless for streaming. Even the servers that Atlas VPN designated as "optimized for streaming" on the free plan could not bypass Netflix or other major platforms. This was consistent with industry norms — free VPNs almost never unblock streaming services — but Atlas VPN's marketing was occasionally misleading on this point. For comparison, NordVPN and Surfshark (Atlas VPN's sister brands under Nord Security) both unblocked 15+ Netflix libraries consistently and supported Smart DNS for devices that cannot run VPN apps. Atlas VPN never offered Smart DNS. ## Historical Pricing Breakdown Atlas VPN was one of the cheapest VPN services on the market. Here were the standard plans before shutdown: | Plan | Monthly Cost | Total Cost | Savings | |------|-------------|-----------|--------| | Monthly | $11.99/mo | $11.99 | 0% | | 1 Year | $3.29/mo | $49.01 | 73% | | 3 Years | $1.83/mo | $71.52 | 85% | During promotional events, prices dropped even further. The 2023 Black Friday deal offered 30 months of service for $49.21 ($1.64/month), and various
💡 Pro Tip: When choosing an Atlas VPN replacement, match the feature you valued most: if it was the free tier, go with Proton VPN Free (unlimited data, no ads, Swiss privacy). If it was the unlimited device connections on premium, Surfshark is the closest equivalent at a similar price point with a vastly better feature set and verified no-logs audits.
### Best Free Alternative: Proton VPN Free Proton VPN is the clear successor to what Atlas VPN's free tier offered, and it is better in every measurable way. The free plan provides unlimited data (no 5 GB cap), access to servers in five countries, and speeds averaging 335 Mbps in independent lab testing. Proton VPN is based in Switzerland, outside the Five Eyes and Fourteen Eyes alliances, and has been independently audited by Securitum. The company is also behind ProtonMail and has a long track record of defending user privacy in court. ### Best Budget Alternative: Surfshark Surfshark starts at around $2.19/month on a two-year plan and offers unlimited simultaneous connections, 3,200+ servers in 100 countries, and a no-logs policy verified by Deloitte. It also offers significantly better streaming support than Atlas VPN ever did, including Smart DNS for game consoles and smart TVs. It is the closest equivalent to Atlas VPN's value proposition, but with a mature feature set. ### Best Overall: NordVPN If you were automatically migrated from Atlas VPN to NordVPN, you received a substantial upgrade at no additional cost. NordVPN is our top-rated VPN in 2026 with 7,000+ servers across 118+ countries, six published no-logs audits, post-quantum encryption via NordLynx, and speeds exceeding 900 Mbps in testing. Former Atlas VPN subscribers who ended up on NordVPN should consider it a win. ### Best for Privacy Purists: Mullvad VPN If Atlas VPN's US jurisdiction was a concern for you, Mullvad is worth considering. Based in Sweden, Mullvad accepts anonymous payment (including cash by mail), does not require an email address to create an account, charges a flat rate of 5 EUR/month, and was independently audited by Assured AB. It is not the fastest or most feature-rich VPN, but it is arguably the most private.
💡 Pro Tip: If you were an Atlas VPN paid subscriber who was migrated to NordVPN, your auto-renewal is turned off by default. Log into your NordVPN account and review your subscription settings before the migrated period expires — you may need to opt into renewal or choose a new plan to avoid losing access without warning.
## A Warning About Fake Atlas VPN Downloads Since the shutdown, several third-party websites have continued to offer "Atlas VPN downloads" or claim to sell Atlas VPN subscriptions. These are scams. The official Atlas VPN apps have been removed from all legitimate app stores. The atlasvpn.com domain now redirects to a closure notice. Any downloadable file claiming to be Atlas VPN is either an outdated build that cannot connect to any server (since all servers were decommissioned) or, worse, malware disguised as a VPN installer. Do not download anything claiming to be Atlas VPN from unofficial sources. If you see ads or search results promoting Atlas VPN downloads, report them. ## FAQ **Is Atlas VPN still available in 2026?** No. Atlas VPN permanently shut down on April 24, 2024. The apps have been removed from all stores, all servers have been decommissioned, and the website no longer functions as a VPN service. There is no way to sign up for or use Atlas VPN. **What happened to my paid Atlas VPN subscription?** All paid Atlas VPN subscribers were automatically migrated to NordVPN with full premium access for the remainder of their subscription period. Only your email address and subscription length were transferred. Auto-renewal was turned off by default on the new NordVPN account. If you subscribed through Apple's App Store, you were refunded instead of migrated. **Why did Atlas VPN shut down?** Atlas VPN's official statement cited insurmountable competitive challenges and escalating costs. The practical reason is that Nord Security — which owned NordVPN, Surfshark, and Atlas VPN — decided that running three VPN brands was not economically justified. Surfshark already covered the budget segment better than Atlas VPN, and the free tier was expensive to maintain without generating revenue. **Was Atlas VPN safe to use when it was active?** Atlas VPN had mixed security credentials. It used WireGuard encryption and included a kill switch, but a critical zero-day vulnerability in September 2023 leaked Linux users' real IP addresses through an unauthenticated local API. The service operated under US jurisdiction (Five Eyes) and never published an independent security audit. For casual browsing, it was adequate. For high-stakes privacy, it was not recommended. **What is the best free VPN to replace Atlas VPN?** Proton VPN Free is the strongest replacement. It offers unlimited data (versus Atlas VPN's 5 GB monthly cap), servers in five countries, Swiss jurisdiction outside the Five Eyes alliance, and independent security audits. Windscribe is another solid option with 10 GB of monthly data and a verified no-logs policy. **Can I still download Atlas VPN from third-party sites?** You should not. Any site offering Atlas VPN downloads after April 2024 is distributing either a non-functional outdated app or potentially malicious software. All official Atlas VPN apps have been removed from the Apple App Store, Google Play Store, and the official website. **Did Atlas VPN sell user data?** There is no evidence that Atlas VPN sold user data. The service claimed a no-logs policy, and the migration to NordVPN only transferred email addresses and subscription lengths. However, since Atlas VPN never underwent an independent audit, the no-logs claim was never verified by a third party. **Who owns Atlas VPN now?** Atlas VPN was owned by Nord Security (the parent company of NordVPN, Surfshark, NordPass, and NordLocker). Since the service has been shut down, the brand is effectively dormant. Nord Security retains ownership of the Atlas VPN intellectual property and domain. ## How Atlas VPN Compared Atlas VPN has been discontinued — but if you are looking at former Nord Security family members, check these comparisons:
  • Atlas VPN vs NordVPN — how the discontinued free VPN compared to its parent company before shutdown
  • Atlas VPN vs Surfshark — another Nord Security product that is still operational and worth considering
## Sources ## Related Reviews Looking for Atlas VPN alternatives? These are your best options in 2026: - [**NordVPN Review**](/reviews/nordvpn) — the market leader that absorbed all Atlas VPN paid subscribers after the shutdown - [**Surfshark Review**](/reviews/surfshark) — unlimited devices, competitive pricing, and reliable streaming from the same Nord Security family - [**Proton VPN Review**](/reviews/protonvpn) — the best genuinely free VPN alternative with unlimited data and no ads
